title: Amazon's Andy Jassy was the one who triggered the Mythos shutdown
subtitle: Reporting this weekend traced the export-control directive that took Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 offline back to a phone call from Amazon's CEO, Anthropic's largest investor.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: Fortune, Politico, Benzinga, and The Next Web all reported over the weekend that the chain of events leading to last week's shutdown of Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 started with Amazon CEO Andy Jassy. According to the accounts, Amazon researchers used a series of prompts on the Mythos-class model to extract cyberattack information that was supposed to be restricted, Jassy raised the findings directly with Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and White House officials, and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick's June 1 export-control letter to Dario Amodei followed soon after. Anthropic was given roughly 90 minutes to pull the models before the directive took effect.

What makes the disclosure awkward is who Amazon is to Anthropic. AWS has invested about $13 billion in the company and secured a $100 billion infrastructure spending commitment in return — Amazon is at once Anthropic's biggest backer, its primary cloud host, and now the entity that triggered a government order taking its newest models off the market worldwide. Anthropic technical staff are reportedly traveling to Washington this week to argue that the bypass Amazon found was narrow and patchable rather than evidence of a broken safety stack.

The episode lands in the middle of a year in which the US has been turning frontier-model access into a national-security instrument: the December 2025 federal AI framework, the AI Litigation Task Force, and the Genesis Mission partnerships all point the same way. What's new here is the role of a private competitor as the trigger. A cloud host with a competing model line, a stake in the developer, and a direct line to a cabinet secretary is a governance pathway no one designed on purpose.

Takeaway for learners: AI deployment is now a three-body problem — the model vendor, the cloud provider, and the government — and the relationships between them can move faster than any published policy. If you're building on a hosted frontier model, treat sudden capability removal as a real operational risk, not a hypothetical one, and design fallback paths to a different vendor before you need them.

sources.title: How a warning from Amazon led the White House to shut down Anthropic's Mythos model — Fortune
Amazon CEO Andy Jassy triggered ban on Anthropic's Mythos AI models: Report — Benzinga
Amazon's CEO reportedly triggered the government crackdown that shut down Anthropic's most powerful AI — TNW
Anthropic staff to meet White House officials next week, Axios reports — US News (Reuters)
